coredata.dc:title: Robust lithium-ion battery state of health estimation based on recursive feature elimination-deep Bidirectional long short-term memory model using partial charging data
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Fengxun
Chen, Shuwen
Ji, Xiaofan
Xu, Jiongyuan
Yang, Mingkun
Xiong, Ran
coredata.dc:description: Accurate perception of the state of health (SOH) of lithium-ion batteries is crucial for their safety and reliable operation. To meet this demand, a recursive feature elimination-deep bidirectional long short-term memory (RFE-DBiLSTM) model suitable for partial charging data is proposed to effectively estimate the SOH of lithium-ion batteries. In this study, the recursive feature elimination (RFE) method is used to screen multiple charging features for obtaining the key features that best represent the SOH under two scenarios with different charging segment lengths. Due to the robust noise-filtering capability and strong ability to capture complex and multi-level temporal dependencies, the deep bidirectional long short-term memory (DBiLSTM) model is used for time series data training, verification, and testing during aging. Experimental results show that compared with benchmark time series models such as long short-term memory (LSTM) and gated recurrent unit (GRU), the proposed method significantly reduces the estimated mean absolute error (MAE) and root mean square error (RMSE) on diverse batteries in the above scenarios. In the scenario for missing partial constant current (CC) charging data, the MAE and RMSE of B0005 cell are 0.0062 and 0.0094, the MAE and RMSE of B0006 cell are 0.0294 and 0.0314, the MAE and RMSE of CS2_36 cell are 0.0510 and 0.0601, the MAE and RMSE of B0029 cell are 0.0057 and 0.0072, and the MAE and RMSE of B0030 cell are 0.0088 and 0.0102. This research innovatively combines the RFE method with the DBiLSTM model to improve the accuracy and robustness of SOH estimation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Recursive feature elimination
Deep bidirectional long short-term memory
SOH estimation
Partial charging data

Analysis of SOH estimation robustness and RFE screening results
Analysis of SOH estimation accuracy in Scenario 1
Analysis of SOH estimation accuracy in Scenario 2

Estimation results of different algorithms under different cells in Scenario 1.
Estimation results of different algorithms under different cells in Scenario 2.
Feature screening results for different cells under various conditions.

Estimation MAE and RMSE of RFE-LSTM on validation/test set in Scenario 1 and Scenario 2.

Estimation MAE and RMSE of different algorithms on validation/test set in Scenario 1.
Estimation MAE and RMSE of different algorithms on validation/test set in Scenario 2.

head.ce:title: Robust lithium-ion battery state of health estimation based on recursive feature elimination-deep Bidirectional long short-term memory model using partial charging data

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Leshan power supply company, State grid Sichuan electric power company, Leshan 613100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Leshan power supply company, State grid Sichuan electric power company

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Leshan power supply company, State grid Sichuan electric power company, Leshan, 613100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu 611731, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu, 611731, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate perception of the state of health (SOH) of lithium-ion batteries is crucial for their safety and reliable operation. To meet this demand, a recursive feature elimination-deep bidirectional long short-term memory (RFE-DBiLSTM) model suitable for partial charging data is proposed to effectively estimate the SOH of lithium-ion batteries. In this study, the recursive feature elimination (RFE) method is used to screen multiple charging features for obtaining the key features that best represent the SOH under two scenarios with different charging segment lengths. Due to the robust noise-filtering capability and strong ability to capture complex and multi-level temporal dependencies, the deep bidirectional long short-term memory (DBiLSTM) model is used for time series data training, verification, and testing during aging. Experimental results show that compared with benchmark time series models such as long short-term memory (LSTM) and gated recurrent unit (GRU), the proposed method significantly reduces the estimated mean absolute error (MAE) and root mean square error (RMSE) on diverse batteries in the above scenarios. In the scenario for missing partial constant current (CC) charging data, the MAE and RMSE of B0005 cell are 0.0062 and 0.0094, the MAE and RMSE of B0006 cell are 0.0294 and 0.0314, the MAE and RMSE of CS2_36 cell are 0.0510 and 0.0601, the MAE and RMSE of B0029 cell are 0.0057 and 0.0072, and the MAE and RMSE of B0030 cell are 0.0088 and 0.0102. This research innovatively combines the RFE method with the DBiLSTM model to improve the accuracy and robustness of SOH estimation.

ce:section.ce:para: Against the backdrop of the energy transition, lithium-ion batteries have emerged as an efficient and environmentally friendly electrochemical energy storage technology. Owing to their high efficiency, long lifespan, and high energy density, they are widely utilized in power storage systems and electric vehicles [1–3]. With the increase in usage time and the number of charging-discharging cycles, the performance and available capacity of batteries inevitably degrade [4–6]. Consequently, accurate perception of the SOH becomes crucial. As a core function of the battery management system (BMS), SOH estimation is vital for ensuring the operational reliability and overall lifecycle cost-effectiveness of the battery [7,8]. By effectively tracking the SOH of the battery, it is possible to prevent overcharging and over-discharging, as well as to indicate when the battery is approaching the end of its lifespan. This enables efficient battery maintenance, replacement, and retirement management [9,10]. This study is motivated by the limitations in accuracy of some existing estimation methods and the inability to address issues related to partial charging data loss. The proposed RFE-DBiLSTM estimation model is designed to be applicable across various conditions, including those having complete charging data, those missing partial CC charging data, and those missing partial constant voltage (CV) charging data.

SOH can generally be obtained through direct measurement or indirect estimation methods [11,12]. For direct measurement, researchers have developed techniques such as Coulomb counting and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy [13,14]. Although these methods have shown good performance in certain applications, their use is constrained by complex experimental conditions and high data acquisition costs. For indirect estimation, current mainstream approaches can be categorized into physical modeling methods, machine learning methods, and hybrid methods [15]. Physical modeling methods typically estimate SOH based on equivalent circuit models (ECMs) and electrochemical models (EMs) of lithium-ion batteries [16–19]. These methods require a profound understanding of the internal characteristics of lithium-ion batteries. For instance, Liu et al. proposed a state-space coupling approach based on the autoregressive equivalent circuit model (AR-ECM) for SOH estimation of lithium-ion batteries [20]. Building on the consideration of cycle number and temperature, Li et al. developed a degradation model based on the single particle model to enhance accuracy, thereby improving the precision of SOH estimation results [21]. Qiao et al. investigated a bias compensation recursive least square-multiple weighted dual extended Kalman filtering method for accurate SOC and SOH co-estimation [22]. However, physical modeling methods have certain limitations: ECMs suffer from low accuracy, while EMs have slow computation speeds. In contrast, machine learning methods, with their ability to uncover battery aging patterns from historical data, have gradually become mainstream. Time series analysis of charge and discharge data has emerged as a key approach to solving SOH estimation problems. The introduction of deep learning techniques, especially in handling complex nonlinear relationships and large-scale data, has further advanced the development of this field [2]. Such methods not only have the ability to learn the intrinsic aging patterns of batteries from large-scale charging-discharging data but also exhibit strong generalization capabilities, making them adaptable to different battery types and operational scenarios.

Common machine learning approaches for SOH estimation include support vector machine (SVM), Gaussian process regression (GPR), and artificial neural networks (ANN) [23–25]. Feng et al. employed SVM to estimate SOH using partial charging segments [26]. Yang et al. validated the effectiveness of the deep GPR model for SOH estimation using two public datasets [27]. A key feature of this approach is its ability to provide the probability distribution of the estimation results. In ANN methods, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) are known for their strong feature extraction capabilities, making them well-suited for end-to-end SOH estimation models. For instance, literature [28] explored a transfer learning SOH estimation model based on a one-dimensional CNN and relaxation voltage. Extreme learning machine (ELM) is a special type of artificial neural network (ANN), and some scholars use its improved models to estimate SOH. Zhou et al. adopted a method named grey wolf optimization-deep extreme learning machine for estimating SOH [29]. The corresponding results indicated this method has the potential to enhance the accuracy and robustness of estimation. Additionally, recurrent neural network (RNN) variants like LSTM and GRU, which specialize in handling long-sequence data, are widely utilized and have been extensively refined for this purpose [30,31]. LSTM mitigates the issues of vanishing and exploding gradients commonly encountered in traditional RNNs when processing long-sequence data by incorporating mechanisms such as forget gates, input gates, and output gates, thereby improving model prediction accuracy. On the other hand, the GRU architecture, being more streamlined with fewer parameters, offers higher computational efficiency. Its modeling capabilities, compared to LSTM, vary across different scenarios, with each demonstrating unique strengths [32–34]. However, above-mentioned single-layer RNN structures may struggle with underfitting or exhibit unstable predictions when confronted with the complexities of the battery aging process. Hybrid methods combine physical modeling and machine learning approaches for SOH estimation. For instance, Xiong et al. estimated the SOH of energy storage lithium-ion batteries by integrating an extended single particle electrochemical model with an improved feedforward neural network [35]. Chen et al. enhanced the accuracy of SOH estimation for lithium-ion batteries by combining a first-order ECM, a fractional-order three-learning strategy particle swarm optimization algorithm, and a backpropagation neural network [36]. The primary challenge with hybrid methods lies in balancing accuracy and computational complexity.

Bidirectional LSTM (BiLSTM) can simultaneously process both forward and backward time series information, showcasing their potential to handle complex time series data and enabling the model to better capture dynamic changes during the battery aging process. However, battery data is often high-dimensional and contains a certain degree of redundancy, making it essential to optimize input data dimensions through feature selection techniques to enhance model performance—an important direction in current SOH estimation research. Recursive feature elimination (RFE) effectively identifies the most influential features on SOH from high-dimensional data, thereby improving estimation accuracy. In practical engineering applications, lithium-ion battery operational data may be incomplete, placing high demands on the robustness of SOH estimation. Thus, developing effective and accurate SOH estimation methods using partial charging data remains a critical research gap to be addressed. This study analyzes and validates the RFE-DBiLSTM model under two scenarios with different charging segment lengths.

This paper innovatively adopts a SOH estimation method based on RFE and DBiLSTM that can achieve effective SOH estimation under various types of lithium-ion batteries and different scenarios, demonstrating outstanding accuracy and robustness. The innovations and primary contributions of this study are as follows:
1)
The introduction of a deep framework within the BiLSTM enhances the ability to handle complex nonlinear relationships and capture dependencies in long time series data.
2)
By integrating the RFE method with the DBiLSTM model, precise extraction of key battery features and efficient modeling of bidirectional time series are achieved, addressing the challenges of feature selection in traditional SOH estimation methods. This integration reduces the training burden on the model and improves estimation accuracy.
3)
Under various charging segment scenarios and different lithium-ion batteries, the developed method is compared with other benchmark time series algorithms using MAE and RMSE, demonstrating that the RFE-DBiLSTM model is more effective in addressing SOH estimation issues.

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Section 2 presents the mathematical analysis, including the feature extraction, the RFE method, the DBiLSTM modelling, and the framework for the RFE-DBiLSTM estimation model. Section 3 discusses the description of experimental data, validation of the effectiveness for the RFE method, and evaluations of SOH estimation. Finally, Section 4 concludes the paper and provides future perspectives.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feature extraction based on charging segments
ce:section.ce:para: In order to improve the accuracy of the SOH estimation model for lithium-ion batteries, this research adopts a feature extraction method based on charging curves. By analyzing the changes in the voltage and current during the battery charging process, the characteristic information related to battery aging can be effectively identified [37]. Six key features related to SOH are selected from the charging segments to capture the dynamic changes in battery performance, including the constant current charging time (CCCT), the constant voltage charging time (CVCT), the mean current of the CV segment, the mean voltage of the CC segment, the slope of the constant current charging voltage- constant current charging time (CCCV-CCCT) segment, and the slope of the contant voltage charging current- constant voltage charging time (CVCC-CVCT) segment. These features are represented by F1, F2, F3, F4, F5, and F6, respectively. They provide important support for in-depth research on the battery aging process. In the B0005 cell from the NASA public dataset [38], the geometric representations of these features are shown in Fig. 1.

Specifically, each feature encapsulates critical information that reflects the battery internal state during charging. For example, CCCT and CVCT represent the durations of constant current and constant voltage phases, which are indicative of the ability to sustain charge under different conditions as it ages. F3 and F4 capture the average performance of the battery in each phase, offering insights into how current and voltage behaviors shift over time with usage. The slopes of F5 and F6 quantify the rate of change, highlighting degradation trends that might not be visible through duration or mean values alone. These features together provide a comprehensive view of the battery health by describing time, mean, and rate of change dynamics across charging phases. To mitigate the impact of data magnitude on subsequent models and improve model convergence speed, it is essential to perform dimensionless processing on the aforementioned features. This ensures that the model is not biased toward features with larger numerical ranges, which could distort the SOH estimation. results.Min-max normalization is a commonly used method for multi-feature dimensionless processing, and its mathematical expression is shown in Eq. (1).
(1)
x
p
=
x −
X
min
X
max
−
X
min
Wherein, x represents the original feature, X max is the maximum value of the original feature sequence, X min is the minimum value of the original feature sequence, and x
p denotes the feature after dimensionless processing. By applying min-max normalization, all feature values are rescaled to a zero-to-one range, reducing numerical instability and improving the efficiency of training process. This normalization not only accelerates convergence but also facilitates effective feature comparison, enabling the model to assess the relative impact of each feature on SOH estimation accurately. Such normalization is crucial when working with diverse battery datasets or when comparing batteries across different aging conditions.

Recursive feature elimination is a commonly used feature selection approach aimed at gradually eliminating less important features through a recursive process, thereby retaining those that have the greatest impact on the target variable [39]. RFE has particular value in high-dimensional data scenarios, where the inclusion of unnecessary features can not only increase computational load but also degrade model performance due to noise and overfitting. This method is particularly useful for addressing high-dimensional data problems, as it reduces model computational complexity while improving predictive performance. In the context of lithium-ion battery SOH estimation, RFE method can identify the key features that best represent the battery SOH, providing optimal inputs for the model. For SOH estimation, precise feature selection is critical because it allows the model to focus on the most relevant charging characteristics, thus enhancing its ability to capture the subtle effects of aging on battery health. By filtering out less informative features, RFE ensures that the model’s attention is directed only toward the most significant factors affecting battery performance. It performs feature selection by iteratively training a model and evaluating the importance of each feature. The core idea of RFE is to recursively build the model, assess each feature’s contribution, and remove the least important ones until the optimal feature subset is obtained.

In this study, the decision function for RFE is based on support vector machine (SVM). RFE operates by fitting a linear SVM to an initial set of features [40]. The choice of SVM for RFE in this study is motivated by its capability to handle small and medium-sized datasets effectively, as well as its robustness in managing multidimensional data. The ability of SVM to generalize well on limited data is particularly advantageous in battery SOH estimation, where available training data may be constrained. Through iterations, RFE eliminates the least correlated features with a specified step size until the remaining number of features reaches a predefined value. For SVM, the training dataset
{
(
x
1
,
y
1
)
, …
(
x
m
,
y
m
)
}
consists of m points, where x represents the input with dimension D, and y is the output. The goal of the SVM is to find a linear regression function f(x) that returns the true output y, which is expressed in Eq. (2).
(2)
f
( x )
=
〈
w , x
〉
+ b
Wherein, w is the weight vector. Finding

from the minimized norm can ensure the flatness of the f(x) function. This problem can be addressed through the following optimization problem, as shown in Eq. (3).
(3)
min

Wherein, C represents the regularization factor, while
ξ
i
and
ξ
i
*
are slack variables, with ε being a constant. Utilizing Lagrange multipliers, the aforementioned optimization problem can be reformulated into its dual problem for more efficient handling. Consequently, the linear regression function f(x) and the weight vector w can be redefined as expressed in Eq. (4).
(4)
{
f
( x )
=
∑
i = 1

(

*

(

, x
)

i
Wherein,
a
i
and
a
i
*
are Lagrange multipliers, while k denotes the kernel mapping. In this study, the RFE based on SVM regression assigns scores to each feature extracted from the charging curve of lithium-ion batteries, using the square of the i-th element of w as the score for feature i. Features are then ranked according to their scores, allowing for the selection of those with higher values. By recursively eliminating less significant features, RFE effectively reduces the dimensionality of the model, thereby decreasing computational costs, model complexity, and the interference of noisy features.

To ensure the accuracy of SOH estimation for lithium-ion batteries, this study employs a DBiLSTM model. The DBiLSTM model architecture is especially suited for sequential data processing due to its unique structure that captures long-term dependencies in both forward and backward directions. This bidirectionality allows the model to analyze past and future information concurrently, offering a more comprehensive temporal understanding of the SOH. This architecture stacks multiple layers of bidirectional LSTM units, enabling the model to capture more complex and hierarchical temporal dependencies [41]. The deep network extracts information from the input data at various layers, thereby better addressing the non-linear and dynamic variations inherent in battery aging characteristics over time. In the DBiLSTM model, the output of each layer serves as the input for the subsequent layer, facilitating intricate feature learning and temporal modeling through the stacking of multiple LSTM layers. Within each layer, the bidirectional LSTM simultaneously propagates information from both the forward and backward time series, generating forward and backward hidden states. This dual processing ensures that each time step is informed by both past and future context, allowing for more refined predictions, particularly in cases where missing or partial data might otherwise lead to inaccurate SOH estimations.

The existing input data
X =
{
x
1
,
x
2
…
x
T
}
consists of the time series of battery operation data, where x
t represents the input at time t. As a sequential processing model, LSTM is composed of memory cells, a forget gate, an input gate, and an output gate [42]. The specific functions of each gate are described as follows.

The forget gate f
t is responsible for analyzing the current input information x
t and the hidden state information from the previous time step h
t-1 to determine what information should be forgotten, as expressed in Eq. (5).
(5)
f
t
= σ
(
W
f
⋅
[

t − 1
,
x
t
]
+
b
f
)
Wherein, W
f is the weight matrix of the forget gate, b
f is the bias vector of the forget gate, and σ is the sigmoid activation function. The input gate i
t regulates the influence of the current input x
t on the cell state, as expressed in Eq. (6).
(6)

Wherein, W

C is the weight matrix for cell state updates, b
C is the bias vector for cell state updates, and tanh is the hyperbolic tangent activation function.

represents the candidate cell state, which is the new information calculated based on the current input and the previous hidden state. The output gate o

Wherein, W
o is the weight matrix of the output gate, b
o is the bias vector of the output gate, and C
t is the cell state at time t, which is derived from the weighted contributions of the forget and input gates. Unlike traditional LSTM, BiLSTM captures temporal information in both forward and backward directions. For lithium-ion battery SOH estimation, stacking multiple BiLSTM layers enables the model to progressively extract features at different layers, thereby enhancing its ability to track the non-linear aging process. The input data
X =
{
x
1
,
x
2
…
x
T
}
, representing the time series of battery operation, is denoted by x
t at time t. The constructed DBiLSTM model comprises L layers of BiLSTM units, with each layer expressed in Eq. (8).
(8)

Wherein, l represents the current layer, with

and

denoting the forward and backward hidden states of the l-th BiLSTM layer, respectively. Specifically, the forward and backward hidden states of the DBiLSTM are computed as follows.
1)
The first BiLSTM layer receives the original input and generates the forward hidden state and backward hidden state, as expressed in Eq. (9).
(9)

The subsequent BiLSTM layers take the output from the previous layer as input, continuing to generate deeper forward hidden state and backward hidden state, as expressed in Eq. (10).
(10)

t + 1
l , b a c k w a r d
)

The LSTM-based machine learning model addresses the issue of inadequate long-term memory retention caused by gradient vanishing and explosion through the introduction of gating mechanisms [43]. The deep framework enhances the model's ability to represent complex nonlinear features, which is particularly valuable when handling intricate phenomena like battery aging [44]. By integrating the deep framework with the BiLSTM network, the structure of the proposed DBiLSTM estimation model is illustrated in Fig. 2.

Each LSTM layer is capable of progressively extracting more relevant features based on information from previous layers, effectively filtering out irrelevant noise and enhancing the generalization ability [45]. Through multi-layer feature extraction, the DBiLSTM is capable of capturing the temporal dependencies inherent in the input data across different time scales and feature hierarchies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Framework for the RFE-DBiLSTM estimation model
ce:section.ce:para: The overall framework of the RFE-DBiLSTM model for lithium-ion battery SOH estimation, which integrates the deep learning, the BiLSTM model and the RFE feature selection technique, is illustrated in Fig. 3.

As shown in Fig. 3, the main components of the novel SOH estimation framework presented in this paper include the following four parts.
1)
Data acquisition: Observable lithium-ion battery data is collected, including voltage and current data during charging process. These data are sourced from various cycles of battery operation to capture the progressive aging changes over time. Data acquisition is critical, as accurate and comprehensive datasets form the foundation for effective SOH estimation.
2)
Feature selection: Features are extracted from the voltage and current curves during charging, and apply the RFE method to screen the features, reducing the computational load of model training. The RFE method is applied to these features to systematically screen and retain only the most impactful features for SOH estimation, thereby reducing the computational load and enhancing model interpretability. RFE ensures that the model is not influenced by redundant or less informative features, which could otherwise increase computational complexity and decrease accuracy.
3)
DBiLSTM model construction: In this step, the DBiLSTM model, which combines deep learning and bidirectional LSTM layers, is constructed to handle the sequential and temporal aspects of battery data. The model is trained on selected features, allowing it to learn patterns of degradation that are characteristic of lithium-ion battery aging. Training is conducted on a subset of the data, with validation and testing phases ensuring that the model generalizes well across different conditions. The bidirectional LSTM framework within the DBiLSTM enables the model to process information in both forward and backward directions, capturing dependencies and trends that a unidirectional model might miss.
4)
Estimation result analysis: The estimation results of the RFE-DBiLSTM model are analyzed under two scenarios with different charging segment lengths. By comparing performance metrics of MAE and RMSE across these scenarios, the accuracy, robustness, and adaptability of this model are validated. This analysis demonstrates the model has the capability to handle real-world conditions where charging data may be incomplete.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental data and feature screening results
ce:section.ce:para: The lithium-ion battery datasets used in this study are sourced from the B0005, B0006, B0029 and B0030 cells from the NASA public dataset [38], and the CS2_36 cell from the public dataset of University of Maryland [46]. B0005, B0006 and CS2_36 operate at room temperature. B0029 and B0030 operate at a high temperature of 43 ℃. For each cell, the first 60 % data is used for training, the last 20 % data is used for testing, and the remaining 20 % data is used for validation. Additionally, this study analyzes RFE-based feature screening and SOH estimation under three conditions: having complete charging data, missing partial CC charging data, and missing partial CV charging data. Notably, for complete charging data, all six features can be extracted. For missing partial CC charging data, only features F1, F2, F3, F5, and F6 can be extracted. For missing partial CV charging data, only features F1, F2, F4, F5, and F6 are available. The number of selected features for RFE is set to 2. The feature screening results for B0005, B0006, and CS2_36 under these three conditions are shown in Table 1.

As shown in Table 1, the feature screening results for the conditions having complete charging data and missing partial CV charging data are consistent. Thus, these two conditions can be grouped for further analysis. In subsequent analysis, this study refers to the conditions having complete charging data and missing partial CV charging data as Scenario 1, while the condition missing partial CC charging data is referred to as Scenario 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of SOH estimation robustness and RFE screening results
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the machine learning model consists of three layers, trained for 1000 iterations, with the loss function being mean squared error (MSE). To validate the robustness of the proposed algorithm, a comparative analysis of the RFE-DBiLSTM estimation results between Scenario 1 and Scenario 2 is conducted. The specific estimation result curves for different cells are shown in Fig. 4.

In Fig. 4 and subsequent SOH analysis figures, the orange line distinguishes between the validation set and the test set. Based on RFE and DBiLSTM, a comparison of SOH estimation evaluation metrics between Scenario 1 and Scenario 2 is provided in Table 2.

In Table 2 and subsequent SOH analysis tables, the best SOH estimation results for each cell on the test set are highlighted in bold. It can be observed that using the RFE-DBiLSTM model, the estimation results for each cell in Scenario 1 are superior, which indirectly demonstrates the effectiveness of RFE feature screening. Additionally, the MAE and RMSE in Scenario 2 show only slight differences compared to those in Scenario 1, validating the robustness of the proposed algorithm. To explore the robustness advantage of the proposed algorithm, SOH estimation analysis is also conducted for the forementioned two scenarios using two other benchmark time-series machine learning models, and the specific results are shown in Fig. 5.

Based on RFE, when using LSTM [47] and GRU [48] as the benchmark time-series models, the comparison of SOH estimation evaluation metrics under Scenario 1 and Scenario 2 is presented in Table 3 and Table 4, respectively.

By analyzing Table 3 and Table 4, it can be observed that when estimating SOH using these two common machine learning models, lower MAE and RMSE values are achieved in Scenario 1 for each cell, further indirectly validating the effectiveness of RFE. Additionally, under these two models, the evaluation metrics variation between Scenario 2 and Scenario 1 are significantly greater than those based on the RFE-DBiLSTM algorithm. This further demonstrates the strong robust performance of the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of SOH estimation accuracy in Scenario 1
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the high accuracy of the proposed algorithm in Scenario 1, a comparative analysis is conducted between the SOH estimation results of the proposed algorithm and two benchmark machine learning algorithms. The estimation result curves for different cells under various algorithms in Scenario 1 are shown in Fig. 6.

Notably, these algorithms are combined with RFE method in advance. In Scenario 1, a comparison of the SOH estimation evaluation metrics based on RFE and three different machine learning models is presented in Table 5.

From Table 5, it is evident that the proposed algorithm outperforms the other two benchmark methods across both evaluation metrics for different cells. Specifically, on the test set, the proposed algorithm can decrease the MAE and RMSE by up to 164.3 % and 29.7 % for the B0005 cell, the MAE and RMSE by up to 84.1 % and 72.4 % for the B0006 cell, the MAE and RMSE by up to 480.1 % and 369.8 % for the CS2_36 cell, the MAE and RMSE by up to 28.1 % and 14.9 % for the B0029 cell, and the MAE and RMSE by up to 135.3 % and 160.0 % for the B0030 cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of SOH estimation accuracy in Scenario 2
ce:section.ce:para: Correspondingly, in Scenario 2, the SOH estimation performance of the DBiLSTM, LSTM and GRU is compared after feature screening using RFE, highlighting the overall advantages of the developed algorithm. In Scenario 2, the estimation result curves for different cells across various algorithms are shown in Fig. 7.
In Scenario 2, a comparison of the SOH estimation evaluation metrics based on RFE and three different machine learning models is presented in Table 6.

As shown in Table 6, the estimation results based on the RFE-LSTM and RFE-GRU models exhibit significant errors for different battery cells, while the model proposed in this study demonstrates lower MAE and RMSE in SOH estimation. Specifically, on the test set, the proposed algorithm can decrease the MAE and RMSE by up to 454.8 % and 274.5 % for the B0005 cell, 84.0 % and 82.8 % for the B0006 cell, 242.5 % and 234.1 % for the CS2_36 cell, 21.1 % and 16.7 % for the B0029 cell, and 37.5 % and 36.3 % for the B0030 cell.

The analysis of the estimation results for different cells in both Scenario 1 and Scenario 2 illustrates that the integration of the deep framework and BiLSTM effectively captures the hidden information in the time series, demonstrating that the RFE-DBiLSTM model exhibits high accuracy in estimating the SOH of lithium-ion batteries.

Estimating the SOH of lithium-ion batteries using partial data holds substantial practical significance. This study innovatively developed a novel method that combines RFE method and DBiLSTM for accurately estimating the SOH of lithium-ion batteries using partial charging data. This method breaks through the limitation of traditional SOH estimation relying on complete data sets, and significantly improves the robustness and applicability through a two-way time series model. The proposed RFE-DBiLSTM model is evaluated under two scenarios with different charging segment lengths. and the results showed that it has the following significant innovation and advantages. Firstly, the proposed method demonstrates strong robustness. This study illustrates that the model can accurately estimate SOH missing partial CC or CV charging data, highlighting its deployment potential in various practical applications. Secondly, the developed method achieves high accuracy in diverse scenarios. In all testing scenarios, the RFE-DBiLSTM model outperforms traditional LSTM and GRU models in SOH estimation. For all experimental cells, the MAE and RMSE can decrease by up to 480.1 % and 369.8 % in Scenario 1, respectively. In Scenario 2, the MAE and RMSE can decrease by up to 454.8 % and 274.5 %, respectively. Specifically, based on the proposed method, the lowest MAE and RMSE of SOH estimation in Scenario 1 are 0.0032 and 0.0035, respectively, and the lowest MAE and RMSE of SOH estimation in Scenario 2 are 0.0057 and 0.0072, respectively. Lastly, the proposed method possesses broad applicability and practical value. The RFE-DBiLSTM model can be generalized across different battery types and charging segments, making it a versatile tool for battery SOH management. Future work can be carried out in the following research: investigating model applicability under more complex real-world conditions, such as varying temperatures and diverse cycling patterns; exploring the combination of the proposed model with other data-driven approaches, such as transfer learning and federated learning, could also unlock new potentials for comprehensive and adaptive SOH estimation solutions.

Xiaofan Ji: Project administration, Investigation. Jiongyuan Xu: Supervision, Methodology. Fengxun Tian: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shuwen Chen: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Mingkun Yang: Visualization, Resources. Ran Xiong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Science and Technology Project of State Grid Corporation of China (521908230001).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Estimation of SoH and internal resistances of Lithium ion battery based on LSTM network
Int. J. Electrochem. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.N. Van and D.T. Quang, Estimation of SoH and internal resistances of Lithium ion battery based on LSTM network, Int. J. Electrochem. Sci. 18(2023) 100166-100173. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijoes.2023.100166

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Battery SOH estimation method based on gradual decreasing current, double correlation analysis and GRU
Green. Energy Intell. Transp.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Zhang, L. Luo, Z. Yang, et al., Battery SOH estimation method based on gradual decreasing current, double correlation analysis and GRU, Green Energy Intell Transp. 2(2023) 100108-100116. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.geits.2023.100108
